posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: I said I “assumed.” You, Alfert and I are all attorneys, so we know that what we see publicly might not be what happened privately. As an attorney, I see clues, and I can guess from the clues, but they are guesses, educated perhaps, but guesses. I saw evidence of a dispute over ownership of the writings (unlike Galloni, who had clearly prepared in advance for his departure, Neal could not take his work product to Vinous), but that’s compatible with either scenario. All it means is that the lawyers got involved. In the absence of any real evidence, I go with the most probable scenario: he got a more attractive offer from a competing publication and took it. To bring it back to this thread, I have always assumed the same about the reasons William left Decanter for the Wine Advocate.

posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked:
Totally agree with all the praise for the most exciting addition to wine reviewing since…can’t remember(!) - I suppose we have to be thankful for the demise of Mark Squires’ BB, because his contributions here are invaluable. I too took advantage of the TWA 3 month offer, but I will not be continuing. It’s not the quality of the other reviewers so much as the lack of coverage of the areas that I like - Bordeaux and the Loire. Compared to Neal Martin, LPB’s coverage of Bordeaux, irrespective of what one thinks of her palate, is insufficient. As for the Loire, it’s zilch. I did actually write to Stephan Reinhardt, the current “reviewer”, who hasn’t been near the Loire since 2014, to ask if there would be any future reports. I never got a reply. Sadly, some things at TWA never change.

I’ve made my opinion of LPB known before, but even if she were competent, I just don’t understand how they could pull Neal from the bdx beat. His hard-earned depth of knowledge there is exceptional. Given TWA’s heritage (and presumably readership base), you’d think they’d want to showcase their bdx and CA cab coverage.

Anyway, I had no idea William was a pup. All the more impressive

It was a mind-boggling decision.

As the risk of sounding like an ass, her Bordeaux notes are utterly worthless. Every other note has Black Forest Cake, and something soaring, rising like the Phoenix, out the glasses. They are caricatures that have become pure, and appropriate, fodder for John’s bingo thread. Give the region to William, his Bordeaux notes are fantastic. Everyone should read his wonderful exposition on BAMA. Now that is wine writing. Keith Levenberg writes at that level, it’s a gift that frankly, very few of us possess. And like real pornography, you know it when you see it.

You said it, Brother! With a little mike drop to finish…

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I really appreciate Will’s writing and contributions too! Absolutely fantastic.

I’m a bit surprised that so many so hardcore wine aficionados (why else would you be here, if it weren’t for your all consuming passion for wine) have not or haven’t had a subscription to TWA. Even if you don’t have a personal use for ratings of one or the other (or all other) critics, ignoring one of the still most influential voices in the fine wine world doesn’t make sense to me… (and if you believe TWA is no longer influential, is probably a proof that you have disconnected from the market… probably because you didn’t had a TWA subscription in a long time).

I say this respectfully, not sarcastically, but that form of absolutism loses site of the volume of information on the internet on wine these days. And how tied into the market many people on this site actually are. One could almost learn everything one needs to learn from this website alone given the breadth of experience and wisdom here, and not just from William. There are quite a few people on this website that I trust far more than the various critics scattered around the numerous publications.

Whether TWA is influential is an irrelevant point, and has no bearing on whether someone is connected to the market. The only important metric is whether it is relevant to you. You, not the market. I subscribed to TWA for many years. Wine Spectator as well. This was in the 1990s. Toward the end of the ‘90s these publications really shifted away from the consumer and more toward the producers. WS became a luxury rag and TWA became more about the cults and uber-expensive wines - not always but often enough to turn me off. Plus, Parker’s palate became more and more out of touch with a classic palate, venturing more toward modernized, ripe, oaky wines. One could posit that he singularly became the primary reason why Bordeaux took a downward turn away from its classic past and into the era of the modern consultants like Rolland. I had no desire to be a part of that “market”. I was out. Many people here were out, yet remarkably well-connected to the market as a whole. I think you are Way too wedded to the importance or relevance of these publications.

I rejoined to check it out, and support William, but I have no need for any of these publications to keep me connected. Heck, if you like Bordeaux, jump on Jeff Leve’s excellent site for free and you get better connected (plus he’s an active poster here). Leve’s coverage on Bordeaux slays the TWA’s coverage on Bordeaux (and I say that knowing full well that Jeff and I diverge quite a bit on the palate). Them losing Neal on Bordeaux and slotting in LPB does not help them at all. Do you like Black Forest Cake? Would love to see William cover Bordeaux, but he has his hands full!

I think you are Way too wedded to the importance or relevance of these publications.

Hi Robert. I have to disagree with that. As you mentioned, you probably no longer buy that much wine and hence you might underestimate the relevance of the various publications (not only TWA). Here two examples of recently (and very recently) released wines and how the critics influenced the prices and availability pf those wines.

The Sassicaia 2016 got 100 Pts TWA. The average global price of the wine stands at roughly 300 dollars (much above all other recent vintages). Knowing early on that the wine got 100 pts from TWA was fundamental to secure your case for a reasonable price: the importer here in Switzerland sold out in 2 days and you got it for roughly 155 dollars. While availability still is no issue and never was, the prices increased dramatically on the secondary market (below 200 you found nothing, today 250 is the cheapest available).

Same is true for Bordeaux 2019 (at least here in Switzerland): Feeding the Excel sheet with inputs of all critics, do some formula magic, it became instantly clear which wines the superstars of the vintage are (at least for now) and which wines hence will sell out quickly. It was clear that you can wait with your purchase of Lafite or Haut Brion but should hurry to buy first tranches of Mouton, Margaux, Mission or Eglise Clinet, if you want the lowest prices from these first tranches. (one addition: that TWA still moves the market can be seen in the fact that while all six of these wines have almost identical average rating (11-15 critics, Mar 98.6, Mout/HB/Egli 98.2, Lafite 98.0, Miss 97.9), Lafite/Haut Brion were the once which didn’t get into the “potentially perfect wine” range of TWA, while the other four wines did.)

Do you realize how many wines get awarded 100 these days? Once there was a time where 100 actually meant something. Now, not so much. It appears to have influenced price on this wine, but honestly, that means nothing to me. Some of these highly-rated wines, for my palate, are actually as good or better in less-hyped vintages. For example, I just bought a sixer of 2014 Vieux Chateau Certan. Read Mark G’s notes and comments on this wine. Is the 2016 VCC really worth 2x+ more? I did not buy the 2016. Actually, smart money is on the 2011, not really a vintage that the critics went crazy over.

The funny thing is, some of wines that go/went crazy in the gray market (which I have bought for years) - wines like Juge, Gonon, Allemand, et al - get limited coverage by the rags, and their scores are totally irrelevant. I’m lucky to have and have had access to these wines, allocations, that make critics’ coverage irrelevant. Hardly out of touch.

I think you are Way too wedded to the importance or relevance of these publications.

Hi Robert. I have to disagree with that. As you mentioned, you probably no longer buy that much wine and hence you might underestimate the relevance of the various publications (not only TWA). Here two examples of recently (and very recently) released wines and how the critics influenced the prices and availability pf those wines.

The Sassicaia 2016 got 100 Pts TWA. The average global price of the wine stands at roughly 300 dollars (much above all other recent vintages). Knowing early on that the wine got 100 pts from TWA was fundamental to secure your case for a reasonable price: the importer here in Switzerland sold out in 2 days and you got it for roughly 155 dollars. While availability still is no issue and never was, the prices increased dramatically on the secondary market (below 200 you found nothing, today 250 is the cheapest available).

Same is true for Bordeaux 2019 (at least here in Switzerland): Feeding the Excel sheet with inputs of all critics, do some formula magic, it became instantly clear which wines the superstars of the vintage are (at least for now) and which wines hence will sell out quickly. It was clear that you can wait with your purchase of Lafite or Haut Brion but should hurry to buy first tranches of Mouton, Margaux, Mission or Eglise Clinet, if you want the lowest prices from these first tranches. (one addition: that TWA still moves the market can be seen in the fact that while all six of these wines have almost identical average rating (11-15 critics, Mar 98.6, Mout/HB/Egli 98.2, Lafite 98.0, Miss 97.9), Lafite/Haut Brion were the once which didn’t get into the “potentially perfect wine” range of TWA, while the other four wines did.)

If you aren’t buying for investment, the scores are at best a data point to use to decide if you’d like a wine you haven’t been able to taste. And for some critics (Suckling + LBP), not even that.

Totally agree. In my playbook, the best guide is usually a consensus of as many critics as possible. Put all scores into an Excel sheet, calculate the average score, sort by average score and you have a top 10 or 20 list of the supposedly best wines of the vintage. Comparing these top 10 list years later with the actual consumer preference (top 10/20 list in Cellartracker) clearly shows, that this is the best guide for new releases. I would never focus on one single critic (or one Cellartracker review). Consensus of critics or of wine drinkers (Cellartracker), swarm intelligence is the best guide.

I also subscribed to TWA last year, in support of William. My 2 cents:

  • he is an excellent taster, but an equally good (or even better?) writer with a lot of general knowledge about wine - I enjoy reading his in-depth articles a lot.
  • he lives a few months per year in Beaune, which is a great asset compared to certain other reviewers and impacts the quality of his writing.
  • he makes small quantities of wine himself, which is in my book a great way to be an even better taster and be able to ask the right questions when tasting.
  • I don’t care too much about ratings, but his general descriptions of each estate when tasting the new vintages are quite revealing, even if it doesn’t show in the ratings

I was just surfing the TWA tasting notes, and the coverage is very deficient here and the reviewers often change. posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Neal makes another great point about the lack of financial incentive to buy futures. The 2019s may turn out to be an exception, we’ll see, but it’s been a long time since buying futures (for most Bordeaux) made sense economically.

Neal has been the biggest advocate for 375s. He’s dead right, especially for those of us that drink some wine most evenings, where a 375 is perfect. If I could do anything all over again in wine, it would be that, buying perhaps 1/2 of my Bordeaux in 375s. Like Neal, I’m gathering what I can these days but it’s slimmer pickings.

Back to David’s futures point, it’s an interesting quandary. I’m usually of the opinion that the futures game is dead unless (1) you are looking to secure a truly hard-to-source Bordeaux (and not many fall into that camp given production levels; and (2) you want to lock down harder to find formats, like 375 and the big boys. However, I did buy futures heavily for the 2014 vintage. It was my kind of vintage and it was extremely well-priced. While I['m not sure 2019 is my style of vintage - reading between the lines it sounds rather ripe - but were I younger and liked riper Bordeaux, I think that I would hit the 2019 futures game. The pricing has been pretty damn enticing considering where we were in the 2015 and 2016 vintages. And I think some 2017s and the 2018s were even higher.

In terms of futures buying and reliance on critics, it’s a game of chance, to some degree. I’d stick to producers you know well, and make sure that they did not change winemakers or bring in a new consultant, as that changes the wine. Or, stick to a critic whose palate you know very well aligns with your palate. The whole spreadsheet or triangulation strategy looks like an averaging methodology that is prone to mediocrity. Why they hell would I want Suckling or LPB in that formula? They heavily skew the result in a bad direction. In truth, often-times I’d just rather wait to try it, and so what, I may pay more. I’d rather pay more and be right, then get a deal and be wrong.

I see GregK responded. He has a killer, but like me, very specific palate. My sentiments are 100% aligned with his.

If you have a universal palate, or are getting into wine, this law of averages approaches likely works. If you have a very specific palate - for example, one that prefers classic Bordeaux over modern Bordeaux - this approach does not work at all.

I can give you loads of examples. GregK highlights 2009 Cos. I’ll highlight 2010 Troplong Mondot an example. Parker rated it a 99. The critics seem to range between 94-99, so a 96.5 average. CT gives it approximately a 94-point average rating among the CT users. Sounds like a winner, right? It’s a monstrosity of a wine, clocking in at 16%. It’s grotesque, nothing like Bordeaux. Yet, waves and waves of posters on CT love on it like the critics do. Perhaps anyone applying the law of averages would love this wine. I despise this wine. I notice my friend Tooch - a guy with a golden palate - is one of the few dissenters on CT, rated it a 71 and next to him, someone else that I know, gives it an 83, calling it “Totally overblown, ridiculous wine.”. His 83-rating was generous. Their reviews are dead right, for my palate. The average scores, dead wrong, for my palate. Pavie, Bellevue Mondotte, Lascombes, Smith Haut Lafite, Pape Clemente, et al. Lots of them run like this.

So that’s the other side of the view.

So that’s the other side of the view.

True, true. You should know what you like and don’t like. Which style of wine, which appellation, etc. That’s the filter you put over the grid of scores. For Bordeaux I put an ABV filter on top of the average scores (the lower the better), I exclude all wines from Gerard Perse (Pavie, etc.), I make a weighted average and let that flow into the calculation (with more weight of more aligned palates like Neil Martin, JM Quarin), availability is another layer you put on top (being it for hard to get wines, once released or hard to get formats), then another layer of what you have in your cellar (you probably don’t want only Pauillacs but some variety), then the layer of what financially is possible… of course it’s not that simple, but its still a damn good tool to buy the best wines.

As mentioned before, the top 10 or 20 lists based on these averages are the best guide. More so than the score itself. Like low ABV wines? Browse through the top 10 list for these wines. Like only this list of wineries? Browse through the top 10 list of these wineries. This method or the Cellartracker averages are not the secret sauce to Wine heaven without disappointments, but you will drink better, more complex and harmonious wines… on average.

And these single wines examples you and david mentioned: Of course there will always be such examples. On Cellartracker and with the critics. But there are is so much more and broader empirical evidence to the other side. (again seen just recently at a 64 wine 2010 Bdx retrospective, all blind tasting: the best 10 wines of the tasting have the following cellartracker positions: 3, 4, 2, 5, 139, 1, 8 (with the three missing wines easily qualifying for top 10 on cellartracker but excluded here as not enough reviews on cellartracker-> le pin, latour, lafleur).

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Buying wine is tough. Few of us, no matter where we live, are able to regularly attend tastings. In the past, we had to rely on one or two critics, which wasn’t a great system, since their taste evolves as our own one does. Now, we have a plethora of views for Bordeaux, for example. But that hasn’t meant there has been an improvement - because my taste is mine, yours is yours and theirs is theirs. When I started out, I used to shop around for a consensus too and as a result, bought some good wine - but a lot of total rubbish too. Lots of critics liked Fombrauge and Lascombes!

Nowadays, the only Bordeaux critic I follow is Neal Martin, simply because I know from experience that our tastes are quite similar, but even then I have to be careful. Including the views of tasters like LPB in any buying decision would for me be total lunacy! I do look on CT, there are one or two people whose tastes I respect, but frankly deciphering who is hiding behind Joey69 is beyond me and knowing that a whole load of Joey69s like a bottle is hardly a data point. Also, you have to question their motives - not everyone who has paid a fortune for a wine is going to admit they screwed up - and they might just want to be positive to sell the remaining 11 at auction!

Actually, if I was starting buying Bordeaux from scratch today with a decent budget, I would focus mainly on buying older wines at auction. Unlike with young wines, this is where a consensus stands a better chance of working. The older a wine is, the more we all tend to agree if it’s good. It’s far easier to buy at auction than in the past - especially if you are based in Europe. Of course the prices are not necessarily the best compared to release prices - but the money saved on buying cases of loathsome stuff that most critics like and I hate would easily cover the difference. In fact, I wouldn’t buy young Bordeaux at all - the chances of saving money are diminishing with every year. Why not let someone else cellar the stuff? I’ve been backfilling at auction every month for the last three years and of the 1500 or so bottles I’ve bought, I’ve had two duds. So much for the risks of poor cellaring.

Filling up your cellar with young wines also carries a risk - what happens when your taste changes, which it probably will? All those years spent waiting for stuff which in the end is no longer what you actually like. Been there, done that, got the T-shirt!

But each to his own!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
To pull this thread back to Back to William… what do you think about his Bordeaux palate and would you buy based on his rating?

Never having had a Tertre Roteboeuf, I Picked up several bottles of 2010 based on his CT note (100 points!), and I’m curious if you would do the same, given your general aversion to St Emilion and 100% new oak.

I have a case of bottles and another of magnums of that wine, so my money is at least where my mouth is! Mitjaville wines are certainly not shy, but they are in their own stylistic universe and don’t really have much in common—aside from coming from Saint-Emilion and seeing 100% new oak—with some of their “modernist” neighbors. François does pretty much everything his own idiosyncratic way, from pruning to vinification to élevage; and he is a very thoughtful, interesting man to talk to. That note was written when I was a graduate student, and today, I actually find myself gravitating as much towards the less powerful vintages of Tertre Roteboeuf (e.g. 1999, 2001, 2006) as I do to the more muscular ones (1998 is probably showing the best of all today). I might think of opening a bottle of 2010 in another five years or so. I have some 2010 Roc de Cambes which I’m using as something of a stalking horse for the evolution of the 2010 Tertre Roteboeuf.

But I am pretty happy to be specializing in Burgundy and Champagne, I must say. That already makes for 25,000 km on the roads of France and over 4,000 wines per year, which is more than enough for me. So I’m afraid I have to disclaim any aspiration to cover Bordeaux (even if I’ll continue to enjoy the region’s wines in private).

It seems more designed to get a more accurate review by the method of large numbers. Let’s assume that half of all bordeaux critics were clones of Jeff Leve and the other half of Robert Alfert. Averaging there responses would just give you the response of no one at all. Adding in a hundred more differing viewpoints only complicates the process.

I think CellarTracker is an extremely useful tool (and I’m a happily paying customer), but not for the aggregate score feature. For something as subjective as wine, the wisdom of the crowd is often meaningless imho.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
That’s outstanding - William, humor me; what’s your guilty pleasure high octane wine - you know, the way Alfert secretly adores Ovid? I’m genuinely curious…

Ha, that’s a good question!

I would say that my palate is pretty consistent, so there are not going to be too many surprises. I certainly have quite a broad palate within the parameters of what I consider balanced: so while I love the racy acid-driven tension and aromatic range of cooler Burgundy vintages, I can certainly find things more things to admire than some of my friends in the best renditions of much hotter, riper vintages such as 2003. (One question I ponder a lot is why so few 2003s turned out as well as so many 1947s? That is a question Burgundy as a whole is going to have to figure out in the coming decade).

In terms of my personal evolution as a taster, I cut my teeth on very classic stuff: old Bordeaux and old Mosel, getting into Burgundy over time. But I definitely had a few years where I was very interested in “cult” California and the contemporary southern Rhône; I think because the texture and muscle of those wines, especially when young, was such a contrast to what I had formed my palate on. It was familiarity with those high octane, super-ripe wines that led me back to where I had started, which was very classically balanced, mature wines—and I returned to the latter with a much more conscious conception of the seemingly rather abstract qualities of balance, elegance, and finesse precisely because of my intimate experience with wines that didn’t really manifest much of those things.

As for wines that I appreciate a lot that are not really “AFWE-approved”, I’d say I’m a big fan of white Rayas, which when old is an extraordinary gastronomic wine to my palate, pairing well with many of the most delicious, cardiac-arresting components of classical French cooking. But I have many friends who hate that wine. I also really like the wines of Domaine de la Bongran in Viré-Clessé, which generally have a bit of residual sugar as well as botrytis influence, to say nothing of >14% alcohol. (I sometimes reflect that it’s a measure of how impoverished the vocabulary of wine tasting is that, on paper, descriptions of Bongran and Rombauer Chardonnay have a lot in common; yet the wines are totally different in the glass!) To really put myself outside the pale, I’d even happily admit that I wouldn’t turn down a glass of Helen Turley’s 1994 Pahlmeyer Merlot!

But I don’t think anyone looking at my cellar inventory would find anything that’s out of place with what I write; because I try to write about the wines I like; and I buy, within the limits of my means, the wines I praise. I still think that “I own 10 cases of wine from this producer” is a much warmer endorsement than any score I could give.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
The Sassicaia 2016 got 100 Pts TWA. The average global price of the wine stands at roughly 300 dollars (much above all other recent vintages). Knowing early on that the wine got 100 pts from TWA was fundamental to secure your case for a reasonable price: the importer here in Switzerland sold out in 2 days and you got it for roughly 155 dollars. While availability still is no issue and never was, the prices increased dramatically on the secondary market (below 200 you found nothing, today 250 is the cheapest available).

Same is true for Bordeaux 2019 (at least here in Switzerland): Feeding the Excel sheet with inputs of all critics, do some formula magic, it became instantly clear which wines the superstars of the vintage are (at least for now) and which wines hence will sell out quickly. It was clear that you can wait with your purchase of Lafite or Haut Brion but should hurry to buy first tranches of Mouton, Margaux, Mission or Eglise Clinet, if you want the lowest prices from these first tranches. (one addition: that TWA still moves the market can be seen in the fact that while all six of these wines have almost identical average rating (11-15 critics, Mar 98.6, Mout/HB/Egli 98.2, Lafite 98.0, Miss 97.9), Lafite/Haut Brion were the once which didn’t get into the “potentially perfect wine” range of TWA, while the other four wines did.)

Funny, very funny.

Actually rather a lot of work and not so funny. And sometimes you don’t have time to do the research and poof, the wine you wanted to buy because you love the winery and buy it every year suddenly costs 30 or 50% more just because some critic(s) awarded it high scores. Annoying… but ignoring the scores doesn’t help here.

I am wary of jumping into this fray considering what I think of scoring wine, especially in the sense you are using them here as if they can be put into a blender and averaged. But I admit I tried to do this sort of thing back in the day too, except I mapped the linear scores onto an exponential scale of sorts. It was all overly contrived and a waste of time in retrospect.

Figure out what you like and what you don’t - producers, sites, vintage character, philosophy, etc. Buy it. There’s no substitute for that. For example, if you like Haut Brion and have the resources, it’s a no brainer every year. It’s basically always good. You don’t need a score to tell you that. Another example - if you don’t like hot vintage wines, don’t buy them, no matter what the scores or hype.
